history.internal_reason: CP-290 covers Claude Code v2.1.94→v2.1.101 (three releases in three days) and reads the changelog interpretively rather than transcriptively. Core thesis is explicit: the release burst reveals two parallel trajectories — security model maturing from surface command matching to shell execution semantics (escape normalization, compound command handling, /dev/tcp gating, LSP injection), and agent architecture moving from anonymous workers to stable identities (named subagents, /team-onboarding, Monitor tool, live agent count). This is an actual read, not a recap. The /dev/tcp ClawdNote adds genuine historical depth (CTF technique circa 2003) that contextualizes why this specific fix matters now. Memory leak section has concrete math (50 MB/hr × 8h = 400 MB) and explains the virtual scroller's self-defeating implementation clearly. Three ClawdNotes land well — MCP leak as Clawd's self-aware joke, /dev/tcp history lesson, named-subagent audit implications. Source is official Anthropic changelog (claude.com/docs/en/changelog), so grounding is solid. Audience is directly targeted: gu-log readers are CC power users who will feel the memory leak fix and care about the security posture shift. No padding, ~1400 Chinese chars of substantive content. Minor concerns: the final 'Default effort = high' ClawdNote appears without prior setup in the main body (the increased default effort isn't mentioned before the ClawdNote references it), which creates a small discontinuity — reader might wonder 'wait, what default effort?'. Worth noting for future stages but doesn't tank the score. Composite math: floor((8+8+9)/3) = 8. Clear PASS, no WARN.
history.reader_friendly_reason: 三天三版本的節奏被讀出兩條軸線——安全模型成熟化、agent 有了身份。不是流水帳，是 changelog 的詮釋。
